title: DHS IG Tells Congress Noem's DHS 'Systematically Obstructed' OIG Investigations
excerpt: DHS Inspector General Joseph Cuffari sent a letter to Congress alleging that Secretary Noem's DHS had obstructed at least 11 OIG investigations, including a criminal probe, by withholding records and revoking database access. The Senate Homeland Security Committee opened a separate investigation in response.
content:
On March 4, 2026, Department of Homeland Security Inspector General Joseph Cuffari sent a letter to key congressional lawmakers asserting that DHS had "systematically obstructed" his office's work across at least 11 investigations.

  • DHS leadership withheld records and placed restrictive conditions on access to information in 10 separate investigations, per Cuffari's letter.
  • DHS revoked OIG's long-standing access to ICE's Enforcement Integrated Database and a database tracking personnel security clearances.
  • DHS General Counsel James Percival warned Cuffari that notifying Congress about the obstruction would constitute "bad faith and bordering on material misrepresentation" — a threat the Senate committee called unacceptable.

Among the obstructed investigations were reviews of ICE's hiring and training, excessive force processes, and a criminal probe. Sen. Gary Peters (D-MI) announced a Senate investigation. Republican Sen. Thom Tillis called it "stonewalling" and "a failure of leadership" during a subsequent hearing where he called for Noem's resignation. The conflict underscored a broader breakdown in ICE oversight at a moment of acute public concern about agent conduct.

"Does anybody have any idea how bad it has to be for the OIG in this agency to come out and do this publicly? That is stonewalling, that's a failure of leadership." — Sen. Thom Tillis (R-NC)
